Fear and Greed is at
15
(Extreme
Fear)
and BTC is at
$63K
this morning. The market just posted its worst week since
November 2022, shedding $390 billion, now at
$2.18T.

The selloff has been broad and fast. But this Monday opens with
a familiar signal: Saylor posted a buy chart on Sunday. JPMorgan
shifted cautious on digital assets. Traditional exchanges are
preparing to challenge Hyperliquid directly. And an AI agent just
booked a hotel using crypto. The market is at its most fearful in
years, but the industry is not stopping.

Let's dive in.

Latest News
-----------

Strategy Is Signalling Another Bitcoin Buy This Morning

Michael Saylor posted his pre-buy tracker chart Sunday
captioned "A good time to add more dots," a signal that has
reliably preceded every Strategy Bitcoin purchase. CEO Phong Le
confirmed the firm's direction in a direct reply.

Any new buy would come roughly 20% below Strategy's recent sale
price.

What did Strategy CEO Phong Le say in response to Saylor's post?

Read More  
( https://link.coinmarketcap.com/f/a/g3XwgvnxVah66wh-ZWO6dw~~/AAQRxRA~/lU3uJcZ6x8vqOA7gavU6_F2ApE0obT0Fob7RDwMlzfQzG__xNBh3GVCSxflZCXqnivxo-JChSVbTyW2xvuuwvhkvtIkIx2XUcdS_SDyh9b5NiDSH7OqFsTzZe_aBPHGKvUWTByq1gvmjgEGwQVPva7HHA8eD2k-rICQOgo4Wvwo~ )
   JPMorgan Just Turned Cautious on Digital Assets

JPMorgan turned cautious on digital assets after Strategy's
dollar reserve fell to $900 mill

74196.subject: The Kim-Putin-Xi love triangle
74196.body:
The Economist’s week ahead

Автор: The Economist


Автор: The Economist

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/kim-putin-xi-love-triangle-the-economist-dpgxe?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BDpaWKwsqQOW7Fc9bdOdArQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0te9PkRZsnWsg1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mq534mmk~oj-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mq534mmk-oj
  
Enjoying this newsletter? Get it in your inbox every weekend, as well as our daily round-up of The Economist’s best journalism, by signing up for free here. Kim Jong Un, North Korea’s nuclear-armed dictator, is at the apex of a love triangle. He and Vladimir Putin, Russia’s president, have lately become bosom buddies. Now a jealous Xi Jinping, China’s president, is visiting Pyongyang to court Mr Kim. That makes things awkward for America, and its aspiration to curtail North Korea’s nuclear programme. Six-wheeled bots may soon be cruising the pavements of your city. We write about an Estonian startup that says that its robots can deliver a pizza more cheaply than a human can. Brooke Unger, Senior digital editor What to expect in the week ahead: ▸ America will publish data on consumer-price inflation on Wednesday. In April prices rose by 3.8% year on year, up from 2.4% in February. Following surprisingly strong employment figures on Friday, investors are nervous about interest-rate rises. ▸ The European Central Bank may raise interest rates at its policy meeting on Thursday. Philip Lane, the ECB’s chief economist, said recently that an “active response may be required” to control inflationary pressures caused by higher energy prices. ▸ The biggest stockmarket debut in history will take place on Friday, when SpaceX, Elon Musk’s rocketry firm, lists on the Nasdaq exchange. The company reportedly hopes to raise $75bn, which would give it a value of $1.8trn. Editor’s picks China and Russia are competing for influence over North Korea When Xi Jinping last visited North Korea, in 2019, international efforts to halt its nuclear weapons programme were still under way. China and Russia, North Korea’s longtime patrons, had backed stiffer UN sanctions on the country as part of an American-led campaign of “maximum pressure” on its leader, Kim Jong Un. Mr Kim had just held two summits with Donald Trump, then in his first term in the White House. And though the second of those summits ended in failure, Mr Xi expressed hope on his North Korean visit that the process would continue, commending Mr Kim on his efforts to denuclearise the Korean peninsula. Nike can’t just do it anymore  Nike, the sportswear company, is struggling with coolness. Gone are the days when its logo seemed aspirational and innovative. “Is Nike Still Cool?” asked GQ magazine in 2024. The

74194.subject: «Менеджер облікових записів»: White Light Digital Marketing – Senior Digital PR & Account Manager

74191.subject: OpenAI is killing ChatGPT as you know it
74191.body:
----------
######                      [Sign Up](http://utm.genai.works/r/gg2ubr5) | [Collaborate with us](https://6hhfxazcbpc.typeform.com/to/uXIl0IFH?utm_source=atlas&utm_medium=newsletter&utm_campaign=general_2026_05_11&utm_content=banner_post)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** OpenAI is rebuilding ChatGPT into an app that runs your day, and it's burying the plain chatbot to do it. Nvidia handed a Korean industrial giant the brains for its robots. And the cost of capable AI showed up twice: Microsoft found seven ways to hijack an agent, and the US is scrambling to keep its best hacking AI away from China.

**In today's Generative AI Newsletter:**

* **OpenAI's everything-app:** Why is OpenAI burying the chatbot that made it famous?

* **Nvidia's AI robots:** How does a factory robot learn to sand a panel with nobody coding the moves?

* **Microsoft's 7 agent hacks:** How do you hijack an AI agent with one plain-English sentence?

* **America's hacking AI:** What happens when an AI finds security holes faster than anyone can patch them?


----------

----------
## [OpenAI is killing ChatGPT as you know it](https://www.ft.com/content/ca0f5f5e-fb9a-41a0-a2a9-0127e15b7db9?syn-25a6b1a6=1)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/07d5d265-c23d-40fe-87ee-da2f90c7c540/Beehiiv_01_-_Jun_8.png?t=1780910950)
Caption: 

OpenAI is rebuilding ChatGPT into a superapp that does your tasks and runs other companies' software to finish them. The Financial Times reports a redesign in the coming weeks with AI agents handling multi-step jobs and partner apps living right inside it. The model is WeChat, the Chinese app that folded messaging, payments and shopping into one screen nobody ever leaves.

The reason is money, and the timing gives it away. ChatGPT has 900 million weekly users but only 50 million pay, and its 2 million business customers already drive 40% of revenue. With a public listing weeks out, that enterprise money is the number OpenAI wants in front of investors. It's serious enough to have sidelined Sora, the video app it launched last year.

An agent that acts for you can turn a small mistake into an expensive one, and OpenAI is betting hundreds of millions of people will let it try. Microsoft promised the same kind of Copilot superapp for this summer and hasn't shipped it, so the first one out the door sets the standard.


----------



----------
## [**How to make AI agents actually work at scale**](https://link.genai.works/FzZK)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/aa1e26f5-3eaf-4322-afa
